title: Every n >= 1 is a product of primes (existence half of FTA)
informal_statement: Every natural number n >= 1 can be written as a product of a (possibly empty) list of prime numbers -- the existence half of the fundamental theorem of arithmetic.
informal_proof_idea: Strong induction on n via the minimal prime factor p = n.minFac: if n = 1, the empty list works. Otherwise p is prime and divides n, n/p < n and n/p >= 1, so the inductive hypothesis gives a prime factorization l of n/p; prepending p to l gives a prime factorization of n.
formal_statement_pp: theorem exists_prime_factorization : ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n → ∃ l : List ℕ, (∀ p ∈ l, Nat.Prime p) ∧ l.prod = n

review.review_basis: repo self-review; kernel-verified through the tracked proof-search loop

notes: Proof authority: Lean kernel via the tracked proof-search loop (episode f53c8d62), kernel_verified on the fourth attempt. Extends the domain's existing `exists_prime_factor` (existence of A single prime factor, via Nat.exists_dvd_of_not_prime2) to a full factorization list, matching LOOP.md's 'strong induction' domain focus. Three real issues hit along the way, all recorded here for future reference: (1) the first attempt bound `hn : 1 <= n` before calling `induction n using Nat.strong_induction_on` and used `rcases eq_or_lt_of_le hn`, and timed out at the full 60s wall-clock cap with no diagnostic detail at all -- switching to `intro n; induction n ...; intro hn` (deferring hn's introduction to inside the induction case) plus replacing `eq_or_lt_of_le` with a plain `by_cases hn1 : n = 1` fixed it (elapsed dropped to 13-46s on subsequent attempts); the exact culprit between these two simultaneous changes was not isolated, so both are noted as suspects for future proofs using `Nat.strong_induction_on` in this environment. (2) `rcases hq with rfl | hq` on a raw `List.Mem` hypothesis (`hq : q ∈ p :: l`) failed with a confusing `subst` error blaming a `List ℕ`-typed equality -- fixed by first converting via `List.mem_cons.mp hq : q = p ∨ q ∈ l` (a plain `Or`) before rcases-ing on that. (3) splitting a `refine ⟨_, ?_, ?_⟩`'s two goals via a flat, unbulleted sequence of tactics silently left the second goal (`(p :: l).prod = n`) untouched even though no tactic error was reported (category `unsolved_goals`, not a tactic failure) -- wrapping each of the two `refine` goals in its own single-level `·` bullet fixed it; flat sequential tactic consumption across a multi-goal `refine` is not reliable in this environment even without nested bullets, always bullet each goal explicitly.
